text:
Q: My colleague suggested I feed TPM values into DESeq2 instead of raw counts because TPM corrects for gene length differences. Is this okay?

A: You should always provide DESeq2 with raw, un-normalized integer counts — never TPM, FPKM, or any other pre-normalized values. DESeq2's core statistical model is a negative binomial (NB) distribution fit to each gene, and the NB likelihood is only well-defined for non-negative integer data. TPM and FPKM values are continuous and typically non-integer, so the likelihood is undefined for them. More fundamentally, DESeq2 handles differences in sequencing depth internally through size factor estimation (median-of-ratios method), which requires access to the original count-scale sampling variability. When you pre-normalize, you remove the count-level variance structure that DESeq2 uses to estimate dispersion; the mean-variance relationship that the NB model expects is destroyed, leading to miscalibrated dispersion estimates and unreliable p-values. TPM also introduces a compositional constraint — each sample's values sum to a constant — which creates spurious negative correlations between genes and can distort fold-change estimates. If your counts come from pseudo-alignment tools like Salmon or kallisto (which report estimated counts with inferential uncertainty), use tximport or tximeta to import them; these packages back-calculate estimated counts on the original count scale while propagating uncertainty, preserving the statistical properties DESeq2 requires. Gene length corrections, when needed for between-sample comparisons, are handled at the import stage, not by feeding TPM into the model.
section: Input data — Why un-normalized counts?

text:
Q: My experiment has samples processed across two sequencing batches and two treatment conditions. How should I specify the design formula in DESeq2, and does term order matter?

A: For an experiment with a nuisance batch variable and a treatment condition of interest, use an additive design: `~ batch + condition`. This instructs DESeq2 to estimate the effect of condition while accounting for systematic batch differences. The order of terms in the formula matters in two ways. First, when you call `results()` without a `name=` or `contrast=` argument, DESeq2 by default returns results for the last term, so placing your variable of interest last is conventional and reduces the chance of accidentally reporting the wrong comparison. Second, factor reference levels — determined by the first factor level in R — must be set explicitly with `relevel()` before running `DESeq()`, because alphabetical defaults can produce counterintuitive references (e.g., 'untreated' may sort after 'treated'). The additive model assumes that the treatment effect is the same in every batch (no interaction). If you have biological reason to believe the treatment response differs across batches — for example, different cell-culture conditions in each batch — you would expand to `~ batch + condition + batch:condition`, but this requires replicates within every batch-by-condition cell and changes the interpretation of the main effects (they then represent effects at the reference level of the other factor). Use the additive model as the default; only add the interaction term when the science demands it and you have sufficient replication to support it.
section: The DESeqDataSet, sample information, and the design formula

text:
Q: Should I filter out low-count genes before running DESeq2? My dataset has many genes with very few reads across all samples.

A: Pre-filtering is strongly recommended, though DESeq2 will not error on unfiltered data. The vignette suggests a minimal filter: keep only rows with at least 10 total counts across all samples. A more principled criterion is to require at least 1 count in at least as many samples as the smallest group size — this ensures the gene is detectably expressed in at least one condition. The benefits are threefold. First, it reduces memory and runtime, which matters for large datasets. Second, it reduces the number of statistical tests submitted to the Benjamini-Hochberg multiple testing correction, directly improving the adjusted p-values for genes that do have adequate counts. Third, it improves DESeq2's independent filtering step: DESeq2 internally optimizes a count threshold to maximize discoveries, and pre-filtering ensures that threshold optimization operates over a more informative distribution of mean counts. Genes with near-zero counts across all samples have no statistical power to detect differential expression; their p-values are approximately uniform under the null, contributing noise rather than signal to the correction. Importantly, pre-filtering should be based on raw counts and should not use information about which group each sample belongs to — using group membership would constitute data snooping and could inflate false positives. A simple rowSums or rowMeans threshold on the count matrix is appropriate.
section: Pre-filtering the dataset

text:
Q: Some genes in my DESeq2 results have NA for padj but not for pvalue. I've heard this is due to 'independent filtering' — what is it doing and when should I turn it off?

A: Independent filtering is an automatic step performed inside `results()` that removes genes with low mean normalized count from the Benjamini-Hochberg (BH) multiple testing correction, then applies BH only to the remaining, higher-expressed genes. The statistical justification is that mean normalized count is independent of the test statistic under the null hypothesis — a gene's average expression does not predict whether it is truly null — so filtering on it does not inflate false positives. By excluding very low-count genes from the BH denominator, the adjusted p-values for moderate- and high-count genes improve, increasing discoveries at a given alpha level. DESeq2 sweeps over a range of count threshrams and picks the threshold that maximizes the number of genes with padj below alpha; genes below the chosen threshold receive padj = NA. The `alpha` parameter you pass to `results()` directly controls which threshold is selected, which is why running `results(dds, alpha=0.05)` versus `results(dds, alpha=0.1)` can produce different sets of NAs. You should disable independent filtering (`independentFiltering = FALSE`) when you have a specific prior interest in low-expression genes — for example, when following up a candidate gene known to be expressed at low levels — or when a downstream tool requires all genes to have an adjusted p-value. Disabling it will typically reduce overall power but ensures no gene is silently excluded.
section: Independent filtering and multiple testing

text:
Q: I want to use apeglm for LFC shrinkage. When is it the right choice and why does it have to be applied after running DESeq2 rather than as part of the main fitting?

A: The `apeglm` method is the recommended default for LFC shrinkage when you want estimates suitable for ranking genes and for visualization (MA plots, volcano plots). It uses an adaptive Cauchy prior — heavier-tailed than the normal prior used by DESeq2's original shrinkage approach — which means it shrinks small, noisy LFCs from low-count genes strongly toward zero while leaving large, well-supported LFCs relatively unaffected. This 'nearly unbiased' property avoids the over-shrinkage of true large effects that a normal prior can produce. The reason apeglm is applied after `DESeq()` rather than replacing it is that it operates as a posterior mode estimator that takes the maximum likelihood estimates (MLEs) of the LFCs — along with their standard errors — as inputs for its iterative optimization. It does not re-estimate dispersions or refit the negative binomial model; it only applies the Bayesian shrinkage step to coefficients already estimated by DESeq2. You invoke it via `lfcShrink(dds, coef = 'condition_treated_vs_untreated', type = 'apeglm')`, where the `coef` argument must be a single named coefficient from `resultsNames(dds)`. Because apeglm requires a single coefficient, it cannot directly accept a `contrast=` vector for arbitrary pairwise comparisons; for those cases, either relevel the factor to make the desired comparison a direct coefficient, or use `ashr` instead. The method is described in Zhu et al. (2019), Bioinformatics.
section: Shrinkage estimation of fold changes

text:
Q: DESeq2 set padj to NA for several genes in my results, and I can see from the cooks matrix that some genes have high Cook's distance values. How does this outlier detection work?

A: Cook's distance measures how much the estimated coefficients for a given gene would change if one sample were removed — it is a per-gene, per-sample influence diagnostic. After fitting the negative binomial GLM, DESeq2 computes Cook's distances for every gene-sample combination. If any sample's Cook's distance for a gene exceeds a threshold (the 0.99 quantile of an F distribution with degrees of freedom based on the model), that gene is flagged as having a potential count outlier. The downstream handling depends on the number of replicates, controlled by `minReplicatesForReplace` (default: 7). If every experimental group has fewer than 7 replicates — typical for most bulk RNA-seq experiments — DESeq2 sets both pvalue and padj to NA for the flagged gene rather than reporting a result that may be driven by a single influential sample. This is conservative but appropriate, since with few replicates you cannot reliably distinguish a biological outlier from a technical one. If every group has at least `minReplicatesForReplace` replicates, DESeq2 instead replaces the outlier count with an imputed value (trimmed geometric mean of remaining samples), reruns the statistical test, and reports a p-value. You can inspect the Cook's matrix via `assay(dds, 'cooks')`. If you are confident the outlier counts are biologically real, you can disable filtering with `cooksCutoff = FALSE` in `results()`, but this risks false positives from high-leverage observations.
section: Handling outliers

text:
Q: For PCA and sample-level clustering, should I use vst() or rlog() in DESeq2? What's the practical difference?

A: Both `vst()` and `rlog()` address the same problem: raw counts have a strong mean-variance relationship (variance scales with mean) that causes high-count genes to dominate distance-based analyses like PCA and Euclidean-distance clustering. Both transformations produce approximately homoskedastic values on a log-like scale so that all genes contribute roughly equally regardless of their expression level. The practical difference is computational and how they handle small-sample noise. `vst()` derives a closed-form transformation from the estimated dispersion-mean trend; it is very fast and the vignette recommends it for datasets with more than approximately 30 samples. `rlog()` applies a gene-wise regularized log transformation that borrows strength across genes using shrinkage, making it more robust when per-gene dispersion estimates are unreliable — which is the typical situation with fewer than ~30 samples. For small experiments, `rlog()` tends to produce better-separated clusters in PCA. For large experiments, the computational cost of `rlog()` is prohibitive and `vst()` is preferred. Neither transformation is appropriate as input to `DESeq()` for differential expression testing — always use raw counts for that. The `blind` argument controls whether the design is used during the transformation: `blind=TRUE` (default) ignores the design, which is appropriate for sample quality-control plots; `blind=FALSE` accounts for known covariates and is preferable when you want to visualize condition effects while removing batch variance.
section: Extracting transformed values — VST and rlog

text:
Q: I have three treatment groups and need all pairwise comparisons from a single DESeq2 fit. How do I correctly specify contrasts in results()?

A: DESeq2's `results()` function provides two mechanisms for extracting a specific comparison. For arbitrary pairwise contrasts among levels of a factor, use `contrast = c('condition', 'B', 'A')`, which returns log2(B / A). The three-element character vector specifies the design variable name, the numerator level, and the denominator level. This approach works regardless of which level was set as the reference during model fitting, giving you full flexibility to extract any pairwise comparison from a single fitted model without releveling and rerunning. The alternative, `name = 'condition_B_vs_A'`, references a specific named coefficient from `resultsNames(dds)` and only works if that exact comparison is a fitted coefficient — which depends on the reference level. For multi-group designs with k groups, loop over all desired `contrast=` calls to obtain all pairwise results; each call to `results()` performs its own independent filtering and BH correction, so if you plan to combine results and control the overall FDR you should be aware that the padj values are not jointly calibrated. LFC shrinkage interacts with contrast specification: `apeglm` requires a single named coefficient and cannot accept a contrast vector, so for comparisons that are not direct model coefficients you should either relevel the reference factor or switch to `ashr`, which accepts contrast vectors. Always check `resultsNames(dds)` after fitting to understand which coefficients are available before choosing between `name=` and `contrast=`.
section: Building the results table — contrasts

text:
Q: I want to find genes that are differentially expressed AND have at least a 2-fold change. Should I filter the results table by fold-change after testing, or is there a better approach?

A: Post-hoc fold-change filtering on a test-against-zero results table is statistically problematic and should be avoided. When you test H0: LFC = 0, identify significant genes, and then filter those by |LFC| > 1, you are selecting the most extreme estimates from a set already enriched for upward-biased LFCs (due to selection at a p-value threshold), which inflates the false positive rate for the effect-size claim. The correct approach is to build the minimum effect size directly into the null hypothesis using `lfcThreshold` in `results()`. Setting `results(dds, lfcThreshold = 1)` tests H0: |LFC| <= 1 versus Ha: |LFC| > 1 for each gene. The `altHypothesis` argument controls directionality: `'greaterAbs'` (default with a threshold) tests |LFC| > threshold from either direction; `'greater'` tests LFC > threshold (up-regulation only); `'less'` tests LFC < -threshold (down-regulation only); `'lessAbs'` tests an equivalence hypothesis. The p-values from this test directly reflect evidence against the effect-size null, and independent filtering and BH correction apply in the usual way. One important caveat: the test uses the Wald statistic based on the MLE (unshrunken) LFC estimates, so you should apply `lfcThreshold` to the standard `results()` output before any shrinkage step. Shrunken estimates from `lfcShrink()` are appropriate for reporting and visualization but not for this test.
section: Tests of log2 fold change above or below a threshold

text:
Q: My plotDispEsts() output shows some genes with open circles sitting above the trend line. What does this mean and is it a problem?

A: In `plotDispEsts()`, filled black circles represent gene-wise dispersion estimates that have been shrunken toward the fitted mean-dispersion trend (the red curve), while open circles represent genes whose gene-wise estimates were flagged as dispersion outliers and intentionally left un-shrunken. DESeq2 estimates dispersions in three sequential steps: it first computes a per-gene MLE dispersion; it then fits a parametric or local trend to the mean-dispersion relationship across all genes; finally, it applies empirical Bayes shrinkage to pull gene-wise estimates toward the trend, reducing the influence of noisy per-gene estimates. For genes where the gene-wise estimate substantially exceeds the trended value — the threshold is roughly 2 standard deviations above the trend on the log scale — DESeq2 flags them as outliers and retains their original gene-wise dispersion rather than shrinking it down. The rationale is that shrinking a genuinely high-dispersion gene toward the trend would underestimate its true variability and inflate its test statistic, increasing false positives. A small fraction of open circles above the trend is expected and healthy; these represent genes with high biological variability. Concern is warranted if a large proportion of genes appear as outliers, or if the trend itself fits poorly (e.g., looks non-monotonic or has a wide scatter around it), which can indicate sample heterogeneity, contamination, or a mismatch between the experimental design and the design formula. In those cases, examine sample-level quality metrics and ensure the design formula accounts for all major sources of variation.
section: Dispersion plot and fitting alternatives

text:
Q: Can I supply TPM or FPKM values to DESeq2 instead of raw counts? My collaborator already normalized the data before sending it to me.

A: No — DESeq2 requires raw, integer-valued counts and will produce incorrect results if given pre-normalized values like TPM or FPKM. The reason is fundamental to the statistical model: DESeq2 fits a negative binomial distribution whose variance is a function of the mean, and it estimates that mean after applying its own size-factor normalization (median of ratios). TPM and FPKM values have already had library-size and gene-length corrections applied, so they no longer follow the count distribution that the NB model assumes. Feeding them in as if they were counts corrupts the variance-mean relationship, inflates or deflates dispersion estimates, and makes p-values meaningless. Additionally, TPM and FPKM values are typically non-integer real numbers; while DESeq2 won't always error out on non-integers, the model is not designed for them. If you only have access to normalized matrices, DESeq2 is the wrong tool — consider a rank-based or limma-voom approach instead. If the raw counts are recoverable (e.g., from a public repository like GEO or the original STAR/HISAT2 output), always go back to those. The DESeq2 vignette is explicit: the input should be a count matrix of raw, un-normalized counts summarized at the gene level.
section: Input data — count matrix

text:
Q: My experiment has two factors: treatment (drug vs. control) and batch (plate 1 vs. plate 2). How should I write the design formula, and does the order of terms matter?

A: Use an additive formula `~ batch + condition` to control for the batch effect while estimating the treatment effect. The order of terms in the formula affects which coefficient DESeq2 treats as the primary contrast returned by default from `results()`: DESeq2 reports results for the last variable in the formula. This is important — if you write `~ condition + batch`, the default result will be for batch, not treatment. Writing `~ batch + condition` puts condition last, making the treatment comparison the default output. You should also explicitly set the reference level for each factor using `relevel()` before building the DESeqDataSet, e.g., `dds$condition <- relevel(dds$condition, ref = "control")`. This ensures the log fold change is reported as drug vs. control rather than the reverse. The additive model assumes the batch effect is consistent across treatment groups (no interaction). If you have reason to believe treatment responds differently across batches — e.g., a technical interaction where one plate had a reagent issue — consider an interaction model `~ batch + condition + batch:condition`, but this requires sufficient replication within each batch-by-condition cell and complicates interpretation. For most balanced designs with known batch, the additive formula is preferred.
section: Multi-factor designs

text:
Q: Should I filter out lowly expressed genes before running DESeq2, and if so, what threshold does the vignette recommend?

A: Yes, pre-filtering is recommended. The DESeq2 vignette suggests keeping genes that have at least 10 total counts summed across all samples as a minimal filter, though a more principled approach is to require at least 10 counts in at least N samples, where N equals the size of the smallest group in your design. The rationale has two parts. First, genes with very few counts carry essentially no statistical power and contribute noise to downstream analyses without any realistic chance of reaching significance. Second, and more importantly, filtering reduces the number of tests submitted to multiple-testing correction. DESeq2 uses independent filtering internally (via the `results()` function), but pre-filtering further reduces the gene universe, increasing power for genes that remain. A stricter threshold (e.g., requiring counts in all replicates of at least one condition) is more conservative but loses fewer truly lowly expressed genes in one condition. The trade-off is sensitivity: overly aggressive pre-filtering can discard genes that are expressed in only one condition. The vignette's recommendation of `rowSums(counts(dds)) >= 10` is a simple, practical default that works well in most bulk RNA-seq experiments. Genes that are all-zero across all samples will in any case be automatically dropped by DESeq2, but removing near-zero genes yourself reduces memory usage and speeds up estimation.
section: Pre-filtering

text:
Q: What is independent filtering in DESeq2, and when would I want to turn it off?

A: Independent filtering is an automatic procedure within `results()` that maximizes the number of statistically significant genes by applying a data-driven count threshold before multiple testing correction. DESeq2 uses the mean of normalized counts across all samples as the filter statistic — a quantity that is independent of the test statistic under the null hypothesis, satisfying the theoretical requirement that filtering not be correlated with the p-value under the null. DESeq2 sweeps over a grid of thresholds and selects the one that maximizes the number of Benjamini-Hochberg rejections at the specified alpha level; genes below the chosen threshold receive NA for their adjusted p-value. This can substantially increase power, sometimes by 20–30% in typical RNA-seq data. However, there are scenarios where you should disable it with `independentFiltering = FALSE`. If your experiment has very few replicates (e.g., n=2 per group), the mean normalized count may not reliably separate detectable from undetectable genes, and the filtering threshold can behave erratically. You might also disable it if you need adjusted p-values for every gene (e.g., for a preregistered analysis plan) or if a downstream tool requires a complete result table. The `alpha` parameter in `results()` directly controls the FDR threshold used to optimize the filter, so changing alpha also changes which threshold is selected — these two parameters interact and should be set consistently.
section: Independent filtering and multiple testing

text:
Q: How does DESeq2 estimate dispersion, and what should I look for in the plotDispEsts() diagnostic plot?

A: DESeq2 estimates dispersion in three steps. First, it computes a gene-wise maximum likelihood estimate (MLE) of dispersion for each gene individually. Second, it fits a smooth trend line — dispersion as a decreasing function of mean normalized count — to those gene-wise estimates using a gamma-family GLM. Third, it shrinks each gene-wise estimate toward the fitted trend using empirical Bayes, producing a final 'MAP' (maximum a posteriori) estimate. Genes whose gene-wise estimate lies far above the trend are flagged as potential outliers and their estimates are not shrunk but left at the gene-wise MLE; DESeq2 marks these with an open circle in `plotDispEsts()`. In a healthy `plotDispEsts()` plot you should see: (1) a cloud of black dots (gene-wise MLEs) that decreases from upper-left to lower-right, (2) a red trend line running through the center of that cloud, and (3) blue dots (final MAP estimates) tightly hugging the trend line, with a small number of open circles above the trend representing outlier genes. Warning signs include: gene-wise estimates that don't follow a declining trend (which may indicate a confounded or mislabeled design), a trend line that doesn't intersect the cloud (possible normalization failure), or an unusually large fraction of outlier genes (possible heterogeneous sample quality). With very few replicates, dispersion estimates will be noisy and the shrinkage will be correspondingly aggressive.
section: Dispersion estimation

text:
Q: I want to apply apeglm LFC shrinkage. What are the requirements, and why is it preferred over the original normal-prior shrinkage?

A: To use apeglm shrinkage, call `lfcShrink(dds, coef = "condition_treated_vs_control", type = "apeglm")`. The key requirement is that you must specify the coefficient using the `coef` argument (a name from `resultsNames(dds)`) rather than `contrast`, because apeglm fits a penalized likelihood model around the MLE estimate for that specific coefficient — it is not designed to handle arbitrary linear combinations of coefficients the way the normal-prior method can. This also means `DESeq()` must have been run first so the MLEs are available. apeglm uses a heavy-tailed Cauchy prior on log fold changes, which produces adaptive shrinkage: small fold changes (likely noise) are pulled strongly toward zero, while large fold changes (likely real signal) are shrunk only minimally. This is more biologically appropriate than the original normal prior, which applies equal shrinkage pressure regardless of the magnitude of the LFC, thereby over-shrinking large effects. apeglm is particularly recommended when you intend to rank genes by LFC for visualization (MA plots, volcano plots) or downstream prioritization, because it yields fold-change estimates that better reflect true effect sizes. The method was introduced by Zhu, Ibrahim, and Love (2019, Bioinformatics). One practical caveat: apeglm is slower than the normal-prior method for large datasets. If speed is critical and shrinkage accuracy is less important, the normal-prior or ashr alternatives are still valid.
section: Shrinkage of LFC — apeglm

text:
Q: I have a three-level factor (control, doseA, doseB). How do I use results() to get the doseA vs. control comparison specifically?

A: Use the `contrast` argument in `results()`: `results(dds, contrast = c("condition", "doseA", "control"))`. The three-element character vector specifies the factor name, the numerator level, and the denominator (reference) level, in that order. The resulting log fold change is log2(doseA / control). This is the most explicit and readable approach for pairwise comparisons among factor levels. Alternatively, you can use `name = "condition_doseA_vs_control"` if that coefficient exists in `resultsNames(dds)`, which requires the reference level to have been set to `control` via `relevel()` before the DESeq2 run. The `contrast` form is more robust because it works regardless of which level was set as reference — DESeq2 constructs the contrast as a linear combination of model coefficients internally. For `lfcShrink()` with apeglm, however, you cannot use `contrast` — apeglm only accepts `coef`, so for arbitrary pairwise comparisons among levels that aren't the reference, the normal-prior or ashr shrinkage types must be used instead (they support `contrast`). When comparing multiple pairs from a multi-level factor, it is best to run `results()` separately for each pair rather than using a loop over results names, to ensure the reference level is correctly set for each comparison. Always confirm the direction of the fold change by checking that `resultsNames(dds)` contains the expected coefficient.
section: Contrasts

text:
Q: DESeq2 is setting p-values to NA for some genes. I know outliers are being flagged — how does Cook's distance work here and what controls this behavior?

A: DESeq2 computes Cook's distance for every sample-gene combination to detect count outliers — samples whose count for a gene is highly influential on the fitted model. Cook's distance measures how much the model coefficients change when a single observation is removed; high values indicate a potential outlier. If any sample for a given gene exceeds a threshold based on the F-distribution, DESeq2 flags that gene and replaces its p-value with NA, preventing false positives driven by a single anomalous count. By default, DESeq2 also performs automatic outlier replacement: when a gene is flagged and there are at least `minReplicatesForReplace` replicates in the condition containing the outlier (default: 7), DESeq2 re-estimates that gene's count for the outlying sample using a trimmed mean of the other samples, then reruns the test. With fewer replicates than this threshold, no replacement occurs — the gene simply gets NA. You can adjust `minReplicatesForReplace` in `DESeq()`: setting it to `Inf` effectively disables automatic replacement, useful when you want to manually inspect outliers. Setting `cooksCutoff = FALSE` in `results()` disables the NA-replacement behavior entirely, returning p-values for all genes regardless of Cook's distance flags — appropriate if you've already QC'd your samples and trust there are no outliers. In experiments with n=2 replicates per group, Cook's distance cannot be computed meaningfully and outlier detection is silently disabled.
section: Dealing with outliers

text:
Q: For PCA and hierarchical clustering of my samples, should I use raw counts, VST, or rlog from DESeq2? What's the difference?

A: Always use a variance-stabilized transformation — either `vst()` or `rlog()` — rather than raw counts for PCA and clustering. Raw counts have a strong mean-variance relationship: highly expressed genes dominate distances and PCA components simply because they have larger absolute variance, not because they are more informative about sample structure. Both VST and rlog produce approximately homoskedastic data where variance is roughly constant across the expression range, making all genes contribute more equally to distance calculations. The choice between them depends on dataset size and sample heterogeneity. `vst()` uses a parametric variance-stabilizing function fitted to the dispersion trend; it is fast and scales well to thousands of samples, and the vignette recommends it for datasets with more than 30 samples. `rlog()` uses regularized log2 transformation with per-gene shrinkage via a fitted prior; it is slower but tends to perform better on small datasets (fewer than 30 samples) where the VST fitting may be less stable. Neither output should be used as input to DESeq2's differential expression pipeline — that must always use the raw counts stored in the DESeqDataSet. Both transformations are blind to the design by default, which is appropriate for QC and unsupervised analysis. If you want to remove the effect of a known covariate (e.g., batch) for visualization only, transform first and then apply `limma::removeBatchEffect()` to the matrix.
section: Variance stabilizing transformations

text:
Q: I want to test for 'biologically significant' differential expression — genes that change by more than 2-fold, not just statistically significant. How does lfcThreshold work in DESeq2?

A: DESeq2's `results()` function accepts an `lfcThreshold` argument that shifts the null hypothesis from H0: LFC = 0 to H0: |LFC| ≤ threshold. Setting `lfcThreshold = log2(2)` (i.e., 1 on the log2 scale) tests whether the true fold change exceeds 2-fold in either direction. The `altHypothesis` argument specifies the direction: `"greaterAbs"` (default, two-sided, |LFC| > threshold), `"lessAbs"` (useful for equivalence testing), `"greater"` (LFC > threshold), or `"less"` (LFC < threshold). The key advantage over post-hoc LFC filtering — running the standard test and then manually discarding genes with |LFC| < 1 — is statistical validity: post-hoc filtering inflates the false discovery rate because you are selecting based on a quantity correlated with the test statistic, breaking the FDR guarantee. Using `lfcThreshold` incorporates the effect-size requirement into the hypothesis test itself, preserving FDR control. This approach uses the Wald test with an appropriately shifted null, and the reported p-values and adjusted p-values are valid under this composite null. Important: the LFC values reported alongside these results are still the MLE or shrunken estimates — they are not forced to exceed the threshold. You should apply `lfcShrink()` separately for visualization. The `lfcThreshold` approach is recommended in the vignette when biological effect size is an a priori requirement for claiming differential expression.
section: Tests of log2 fold change above or below a threshold

text:
Q: My collaborator delivered Salmon output and handed me TPM values. Can I feed these directly into DESeq2, or do I have to go back to raw counts?

A: DESeq2 requires raw, un-normalized integer counts — not TPM, FPKM, or any pre-normalized matrix. This is not a convenience constraint; it is a modeling requirement. DESeq2 fits a negative binomial distribution to count data and performs its own normalization internally using the median-of-ratios method (size factors). TPM and FPKM have already been corrected for both gene length and sequencing depth, which destroys the count variation structure the model depends on.

When you supply pre-normalized values, you violate two core assumptions simultaneously: (1) the input should follow a count-generating process amenable to negative binomial modeling, and (2) DESeq2 needs to estimate size factors from the raw counts to account for library size differences between samples. Pre-normalized values collapse library size information, making size factor estimation meaningless or actively misleading.

For Salmon specifically, the correct workflow is to import data with the `tximport` or `tximeta` package using `type='salmon'`. These functions aggregate transcript-level estimates to the gene level and return estimated counts (with an optional offset for average transcript length differences), not TPM. You then pass this to `DESeqDataSetFromTximport()`. The `tpm` slot from tximport should never be used as your count matrix for DESeq2. Using this import path also gives you the benefit of incorporating uncertainty from the quantification step into the count estimates.
section: Input data — count matrix requirements

text:
Q: I have RNA-seq data from two batches and a treatment condition. How should I build the DESeq2 design formula, and does the order of terms matter?

A: Use an additive design formula: `design = ~batch + condition`. Both variables should be R factors in your `colData`. The additive formula tells DESeq2 to fit coefficients for each batch level (relative to the reference batch) and each condition level simultaneously, so the condition effect is estimated after accounting for systematic batch differences in expression.

The order of terms in the formula affects which coefficients appear first in `resultsNames()` and what `results()` returns by default — the last term is tested by default. However, model fit and statistical inference are identical regardless of term order, as long as the formula contains the same variables. Convention is to list nuisance variables (batch) before the variable of interest (condition), which makes `results()` default behavior return what you want.

This additive model assumes the condition effect is the same across batches (no interaction). If you suspect the treatment effect differs between batches — for example, one batch had a processing delay — use `~batch + condition + batch:condition` and test with a likelihood ratio test against the reduced model `~batch + condition`. Interaction models require sufficient within-batch replication and produce results that are harder to summarize as a single effect size.

One important caution: do not include batch in the design if you have already removed it computationally with `limma::removeBatchEffect` or similar. That double-correction deflates your standard errors and produces anti-conservative p-values. The design formula approach is preferred because it preserves the correct variance structure for DESeq2's negative binomial model.
section: The DESeqDataSet, sample information, and the design formula

text:
Q: Should I filter out low-count genes before running DESeq2? I've seen different thresholds recommended. What does the vignette suggest and why does it matter?

A: Yes, pre-filtering is explicitly recommended in the DESeq2 vignette. At minimum, remove genes with zero counts across all samples — they carry no information. A practical threshold used in the vignette is keeping only rows where `rowSums(counts(dds)) >= 10`. A stricter and more principled alternative keeps genes where at least as many samples as the smallest group size have a count of at least 1, ensuring no gene passes the filter purely on the strength of one highly expressed sample.

The primary statistical benefit is improving multiple testing correction. DESeq2 applies Benjamini-Hochberg across all tested genes. Including thousands of uninformative low-count genes inflates the number of tests without adding true discoveries, making the FDR-adjusted significance threshold harder to clear for all genes. Removing these genes reduces the multiple testing burden and increases the number of significant results at the same FDR level — this is a real gain in power, not a shortcut.

Pre-filtering also speeds up computation, since DESeq2 estimates dispersion and runs tests for every row in the DESeqDataSet.

Note that pre-filtering is distinct from DESeq2's internal independent filtering in `results()`, which automatically optimizes a mean-count threshold to further maximize discoveries. The two are complementary: pre-filtering removes the most hopeless cases before DESeq() runs; independent filtering fine-tunes the boundary among the remaining genes. Do not use pre-filtered TPM values to decide which genes to keep — always filter on raw counts to avoid reintroducing normalization artifacts.
section: Pre-filtering the dataset

text:
Q: DESeq2's results() documentation mentions 'independent filtering' and many genes get NA for padj. What exactly is it doing, and when should I turn it off?

A: Independent filtering is an automatic step inside `results()` that filters genes by mean normalized count and optimizes a filter threshold to maximize the number of BH-significant genes at your chosen `alpha`. The critical statistical property is that the filter criterion — mean count — is independent of the test statistic under the null hypothesis. This independence means that filtering on mean count cannot inflate type I error, making it a legitimate way to reallocate the multiple testing budget away from low-power genes.

Mechanically, DESeq2 sweeps across quantile cutoffs of mean count, applies BH correction at each cutoff, counts rejections, and selects the cutoff that yields the most significant genes. Genes below the chosen cutoff receive `NA` for their adjusted p-value. They are not declared non-significant — they are excluded from the multiple testing pool entirely, because there is insufficient power to detect them even if they were truly DE.

You should disable independent filtering (`independentFiltering=FALSE` in `results()`) in a few situations: when you need every gene to receive an adjusted p-value for downstream meta-analysis or custom ranking pipelines; when your sequencing depth is so high that even low-count genes are reliably quantified; or when you have already applied aggressive pre-filtering and the automatic threshold adds little. Disabling it generally reduces power for typical experiments because more tests compete for the same FDR budget.

The `alpha` parameter you pass to `results()` directly shifts the BH threshold and therefore influences both where independent filtering sets its cutoff and which genes are called significant — they are not independent settings.
section: Independent filtering and multiple testing

text:
Q: What should the dispersion plot from plotDispEsts() look like, and what patterns should make me worry about my data or model?

A: The `plotDispEsts()` plot shows three layers: black dots (maximum likelihood gene-wise dispersion estimates), a red curve (the mean-dispersion trend fitted across all genes), and blue dots (the final empirical Bayes-shrunk estimates used for testing). Interpreting this plot correctly is essential for assessing model fit.

In a healthy dataset, gene-wise estimates (black) are scattered around the fitted trend and converge toward it at higher mean expression, where individual estimates are more precise. The shrunk estimates (blue) sit on or just above the curve — empirical Bayes shrinkage pulls noisy gene-wise estimates toward the global trend, which reduces false positives especially for lowly expressed genes. Genes whose gene-wise dispersion is far above the trend are circled and flagged as outliers; their dispersion is not shrunk, so they are tested with only their gene-wise estimate, which is more conservative.

Patterns that should prompt investigation: (1) Gene-wise estimates systematically below the fitted curve after shrinkage suggest the trend fit may be poor, possibly due to outlier samples inflating the curve. (2) A flat or non-monotonic trend can indicate problematic samples or incorrect group assignment. (3) An unusually large fraction of circled outlier genes may point to sample-level contamination or batch effects driving per-gene variance. (4) If the curve fails to converge on a parametric fit, DESeq2 falls back to a local mean fit — the plot legend will indicate this.

For small experiments (n=3 per group), heavy shrinkage toward the trend is expected and correct — you are borrowing information across genes because individual estimates are imprecise.
section: Dispersion estimation — plotDispEsts diagnostics

text:
Q: When should I use lfcShrink() with type='apeglm', and why does it require the full DESeq() object rather than being a standalone function?

A: Use `lfcShrink(dds, coef=..., type='apeglm')` when you need reliable log2 fold change estimates for visualization (MA plots, volcano plots) or for ranking genes by effect size. The apeglm method (Zhu et al., 2018, *Bioinformatics*) places a Cauchy prior on the LFC, which has heavier tails than a normal prior. The practical effect is adaptive: large fold changes that are well-supported by high counts are shrunk very little, while large fold changes in noisy low-count genes are pulled strongly toward zero. This removes the characteristic 'trumpet' of extreme fold changes for lowly expressed genes in MA plots, which are artifacts of MLE estimation noise rather than biology.

The method requires the `dds` object after `DESeq()` because it needs the MLE LFC estimates and their standard errors as inputs — apeglm maximizes a posterior using these as its starting point rather than re-running the full NB model. This also means you must specify the comparison via the `coef=` argument (a named coefficient from `resultsNames(dds)`), not a contrast vector. Apeglm operates on a single linear coefficient; if you need a contrast that is a difference of two coefficients, use `type='normal'` or `type='ashr'` instead, which accept the `contrast=` argument.

Importantly, shrunken LFCs from apeglm are for effect size reporting and ranking — not for significance testing. The adjusted p-values from `results()` remain the basis for calling significance. Never replace the `padj` column with values derived from shrunken LFCs.
section: Log fold change shrinkage for visualization and ranking — apeglm

text:
Q: I have three conditions (control, drug_A, drug_B) and want a direct drug_A vs drug_B comparison. How do I specify this in DESeq2 without re-running the model?

A: Use the `contrast` argument in `results()` with a length-3 character vector: `results(dds, contrast=c('condition', 'drug_A', 'drug_B'))`. The format is `c(factor_name, numerator_level, denominator_level)`, producing log2(drug_A / drug_B). This works regardless of the reference level of the condition factor — you are explicitly naming the numerator and denominator, so DESeq2 constructs the appropriate linear combination of model coefficients and propagates the uncertainty correctly.

The `name=` alternative selects a single named coefficient from `resultsNames(dds)` and is only appropriate for comparisons that map directly to one coefficient — typically any non-reference level versus the reference level. For example, if 'control' is the reference, `name='condition_drug_A_vs_control'` retrieves that coefficient. But drug_A vs drug_B is a difference of two coefficients (drug_A_vs_control minus drug_B_vs_control), so the contrast vector is required.

Before running `DESeq()`, set the reference level explicitly with `dds$condition <- relevel(dds$condition, ref='control')` rather than relying on alphabetical ordering. This determines which level is the denominator in all default comparisons and which coefficient names appear in `resultsNames()`. It does not affect the validity of explicit contrast vectors, but it does affect the interpretability of `name=`-based calls.

For `lfcShrink()` with `type='apeglm'`, note that contrast vectors are not supported — apeglm only works with single named coefficients. Use `type='normal'` or `type='ashr'` if you need shrinkage on a contrast comparison.
section: Building the results table — contrast specification

text:
Q: Some of my genes have NA for both pvalue and padj in DESeq2 results. I know Cook's distance is involved. What is it doing and how should I handle these NA values?

A: DESeq2 computes Cook's distance for every sample-gene pair to identify count outliers. Cook's distance measures how much the fitted coefficients change if a single observation is removed — a very high value for one sample indicates that its count is driving the gene's parameter estimates in a way inconsistent with the remaining samples. This can arise from library preparation artifacts, sample mislabeling, or genuine biological outliers in small experiments.

The handling of flagged genes depends on the `minReplicatesForReplace` parameter (default: 7). If every condition group in the comparison has at least `minReplicatesForReplace` samples, DESeq2 automatically replaces the outlier count with the trimmed mean across samples and reruns the test — the gene receives a p-value, with the outlier contribution downweighted. If group sizes fall below this threshold (the common case with n=3–4), DESeq2 instead sets both pvalue and padj to NA, which is the conservative behavior you are observing. Genes with NA p-values are also excluded from independent filtering and do not consume any of the FDR budget.

To investigate: use `plotCounts(dds, gene=..., intgroup='condition')` on flagged genes to visualize whether one sample's count is genuinely aberrant. If you are confident the high count reflects real biology rather than technical error, you can pass `cooksCutoff=FALSE` to `results()` to suppress this behavior — but do so with care, as it can inflate false positives from corrupted data. Alternatively, if you have 5–6 replicates per group, lowering `minReplicatesForReplace` to match your actual group size enables automatic replacement rather than NA assignment.
section: Dealing with outliers — Cook's distance and minReplicatesForReplace

text:
Q: I need to run PCA and generate a heatmap of my 80-sample RNA-seq dataset. Should I use vst() or rlog() in DESeq2, and does it matter?

A: For a dataset of 80 samples, `vst()` is the correct choice. The DESeq2 vignette recommends `vst()` for datasets with more than roughly 30 samples because it is substantially faster computationally while producing results that are virtually equivalent to rlog in practice for larger datasets. Both transformations produce approximately homoscedastic values suitable for PCA, hierarchical clustering, heatmaps, and other distance-based analyses. Neither should be used as input to DESeq2's differential expression testing — they are exploratory analysis tools only.

`vst()` works by estimating the dispersion-mean trend from the data and applying a variance-stabilizing transformation derived from that trend, equalizing variance across the mean-count range. `rlog()` uses regularized log transformation with empirical Bayes shrinkage toward the gene-wise mean — it applies heavier correction to low-count genes and handles wide variation in library sizes more gracefully. This makes rlog preferable for small experiments (fewer than ~30 samples), particularly when library sizes vary substantially between samples, where low-count genes would otherwise dominate PCA axes.

Both functions have a `blind` argument (default: `TRUE`) that estimates dispersion parameters without reference to the experimental design — appropriate for unsupervised QC where you do not want design information to influence the transformation. Set `blind=FALSE` if your samples have very different biological compositions (e.g., tumor vs normal), as blind estimation can understate biological variance and compress meaningful separation in PCA. For your 80-sample dataset, `vst(dds, blind=TRUE)` is the practical and recommended starting point.
section: Data transformations and visualization — vst and rlog

text:
Q: I want to call genes that show a 'biologically meaningful' fold change, not just statistically significant ones. Is it valid to filter results by |LFC| > 1 after running DESeq2?

A: Post-hoc LFC filtering is statistically problematic and the DESeq2 vignette offers a proper alternative: the `lfcThreshold` parameter in `results()`. Setting `results(dds, lfcThreshold=1, altHypothesis='greaterAbs')` changes the null hypothesis from H0: LFC=0 to H0: |LFC| ≤ 1. DESeq2 then performs a Wald test against the threshold boundaries (testing whether the true LFC exceeds 1 in absolute value), and the p-value properly reflects uncertainty about whether the true effect clears your biological cutoff.

Post-hoc filtering by |LFC| > 1 on unshrunken MLE estimates selects on a noisy quantity without accounting for estimation uncertainty. A gene with a true LFC near zero but high variance can produce MLE estimates above 1 by chance and pass the filter. The `lfcThreshold` approach accounts for this uncertainty — a gene with high variance needs its confidence interval to clear the threshold, not just its point estimate.

The `altHypothesis` options are: `'greaterAbs'` (two-sided, default — |LFC| > threshold), `'lessAbs'` (LFC within the threshold, useful for equivalence testing), `'greater'` (LFC > threshold, one-sided), and `'less'` (LFC < -threshold). Note that `lfcThreshold` uses MLE (unshrunken) LFC for the hypothesis test — shrunken LFCs from `lfcShrink()` are for reporting and ranking, not for this test. The threshold should reflect actual biological context: 1 (2-fold) is common but may be inappropriate for subtle phenotypes where smaller effects are functionally meaningful.
section: Tests of log fold change above a threshold — lfcThreshold and altHypothesis

text:
Q: My collaborator handed me a TPM matrix from Salmon. Can I just plug that into DESeq2's DESeqDataSetFromMatrix() and run the standard pipeline?

A: No — DESeq2 requires raw, un-normalized integer counts, and using TPM or FPKM will silently corrupt the analysis. DESeq2's statistical model is a negative binomial (NB) distribution parameterized by a mean and a dispersion term. The NB model assumes that variance scales with the mean in a specific way that holds for raw counts. When you supply TPM, two things go wrong: first, the values are no longer integers, so the discrete probability model is misapplied; second, TPM normalization has already divided by gene length and library size, removing the count variation that DESeq2's size-factor estimation is designed to handle. The median-of-ratios normalization DESeq2 computes internally will then be estimating factors on top of an already-normalized matrix, producing nonsensical corrections. The downstream dispersion estimates and Wald test statistics will be invalid. The correct approach is to import the raw estimated counts from Salmon using tximeta or tximport with type='salmon', which brings in the counts (not TPM) along with any necessary offset corrections for transcript-length bias. If you only have a TPM matrix and no access to the raw counts, DESeq2 is the wrong tool; consider a method designed for normalized inputs.
section: Input data — Count matrix input

text:
Q: I have an RNA-seq experiment with two conditions (treated vs. control) collected across three batches. Should my design formula be ~condition, ~batch+condition, or ~batch*condition?

A: The right choice depends on whether you expect batch to interact with treatment effect. For most experiments, ~batch + condition (additive) is appropriate: it tells DESeq2 to estimate a batch coefficient and a condition coefficient independently, effectively controlling for batch while still testing the condition effect across all samples. The variable listed last in the formula — condition here — is the one DESeq2 will build default result contrasts for, which is usually what you want. An interaction model, ~batch + condition + batch:condition (or equivalently ~batch*condition), tests whether the treatment effect itself differs between batches. This is rarely the primary biological question; it's more useful for diagnosing whether your batches are so confounded with condition that the additive assumption fails. Interpreting interaction coefficients is also more complex: the main-effect terms become the effect within the reference batch only, not the average effect. A common mistake is to use ~condition alone when batch is a real source of variance — this inflates dispersion estimates and loses power. Include batch in the design whenever samples were processed in distinct groups that are not the biological variable of interest. If batch is completely confounded with condition (e.g., all treated samples were in one batch), no formula can rescue the design; you need to collect new data.
section: Multi-factor designs

text:
Q: Why should I pre-filter low-count genes before running DESeq2, and what threshold does the vignette suggest?

A: Pre-filtering removes genes that have virtually no chance of being called differentially expressed, which improves statistical power for the genes that remain. DESeq2 applies Benjamini-Hochberg multiple testing correction across all genes that receive a p-value. The more tests you perform, the larger the penalty paid to control FDR. Genes with near-zero counts across all samples contribute tests but almost never survive any significance threshold; they simply dilute the pool and force the FDR correction to be more stringent for genuinely expressed genes. The vignette recommends keeping rows where the sum of counts across all samples is at least 10 — a conservative but defensible cutoff. A stricter heuristic is to require at least 1 count in the smallest group size worth of samples (e.g., if you have 3 treated samples, keep genes with ≥ 3 counts total). Neither cutoff needs to be precise because DESeq2's independent filtering step (which runs automatically inside results()) will additionally remove low-count genes from the multiple testing burden using a data-adaptive threshold. Pre-filtering is therefore a coarse, upstream step that speeds up computation and reduces memory use; independent filtering is the statistically principled refinement. Over-aggressive pre-filtering (e.g., requiring RPKM > 1) can remove real genes and is not recommended — stick to simple count sums.
section: Pre-filtering the dataset

text:
Q: When should I use apeglm shrinkage instead of the older 'normal' prior, and why does lfcShrink() with apeglm require the coef= argument rather than contrast=?

A: apeglm (Zhu et al. 2019, Bioinformatics) is the recommended default for LFC shrinkage in DESeq2 when you want to rank or visualize genes by effect size. It uses an adaptive Cauchy-like prior that is heavy-tailed: genuinely large log-fold changes are allowed to remain large, while small or noisy LFCs are pulled toward zero. The older 'normal' prior uses a zero-centered normal distribution, which is symmetric and can over-shrink genes with truly large effects because the prior places relatively little mass in the tails. The practical consequence is that apeglm-shrunken LFCs are better calibrated for MA plots, volcano plots, and ranked gene lists. The coef= requirement exists because apeglm operates directly on a single coefficient from the model matrix — it refits a penalized GLM for that coefficient using the MLE estimates already computed by DESeq(). The contrast= interface constructs arbitrary linear combinations of coefficients (e.g., a difference-of-differences in an interaction model), which apeglm cannot handle because it needs a single, identifiable coefficient axis to place the prior on. If your comparison of interest maps cleanly to one coefficient name from resultsNames(dds), use apeglm with coef=. If you need a contrast that doesn't correspond to a single coefficient, use type='ashr' instead, which does support the contrast= interface.
section: LFC shrinkage — apeglm

text:
Q: How does DESeq2 estimate size factors, and when does the default method break down?

A: DESeq2 uses the median-of-ratios method introduced by Anders and Huber (2010). For each gene, a pseudo-reference count is computed as the geometric mean across all samples. Each sample's size factor is the median of the ratios of its counts to those pseudo-references across all genes. The geometric mean is undefined for any gene that has a zero count in at least one sample, so those genes are excluded from the calculation. The median is robust to a minority of differentially expressed genes and to zeros in the remaining genes. This method breaks down in two situations. First, in very sparse data — such as single-cell RNA-seq or metagenomic datasets — so many genes have zero counts in individual samples that there are few or no valid ratios to take a median over. The workaround is estimateSizeFactors(dds, type='poscounts'), which uses a modified approach based on non-zero counts, or to use size factors from a dedicated tool like scran. Second, if the library composition is highly skewed — for example, a few extremely highly expressed genes dominate the library in some samples — the median-of-ratios estimate can be distorted. In such cases, providing spike-in–derived size factors or using a within-condition reference sample is more appropriate than relying on the default.
section: Normalization — size factor estimation

text:
Q: How does DESeq2's dispersion estimation work, and what should I look for in a plotDispEsts() diagnostic plot?

A: DESeq2 estimates dispersion in three stages. First, it computes a gene-wise maximum likelihood estimate (MLE) of dispersion for each gene independently. These estimates are noisy, especially for genes with few counts. Second, it fits a trend line — either parametric (default) or locally-weighted — to the dispersion-mean relationship across all genes. Third, it shrinks each gene-wise estimate toward the trend using a maximum a posteriori (MAP) approach, weighting by how much information is available for each gene. Genes whose gene-wise dispersion lies far above the trend line are flagged as potential outliers; for these, the gene-wise estimate is used instead of the shrunk estimate to avoid under-estimating variance and inflating false positives. In a healthy plotDispEsts() plot, you expect to see: a cloud of black gene-wise estimates scattered around the fitted trend line (red curve), with the trend showing higher dispersion at low mean counts and lower dispersion at high mean counts; and final MAP estimates (blue dots) pulled toward the trend, with a subset of high-dispersion outlier genes circled. Warning signs include: a trend line that clearly misses the bulk of the cloud (suggesting the parametric fit failed — try fitType='local'); MAP estimates that barely move from gene-wise estimates (too little shrinkage, possibly too few samples); or all estimates clustered at the upper bound (severe overdispersion, possibly model misspecification or contamination).
section: Dispersion estimation

text:
Q: What is the difference between using contrast=c('condition','treated','control') versus name= in results(), and when is each appropriate?

A: Both arguments specify which comparison to extract from the fitted DESeq2 model, but they differ in flexibility and use case. contrast=c('condition', 'treated', 'control') constructs a contrast explicitly: it computes log2(treated / control) as the difference of the two corresponding model coefficients, and it works regardless of which level was set as the reference in the factor. This is the safest approach when you have multiple factor levels and want to ensure you're comparing exactly the right pair. It also handles cases where the reference level differs from what you want as the denominator. name= accepts a coefficient name from resultsNames(dds) — for example, 'condition_treated_vs_control' — and extracts that coefficient directly. It is simpler and unambiguous when the comparison is already encoded as a single coefficient, and it is required for shrinkage with apeglm. For interaction models, the coefficient names encode the interactions (e.g., 'conditiontreated.batchB'), and name= is the only way to extract those specific terms; contrast= cannot express them as simple differences. A practical rule: use contrast= for straightforward pairwise comparisons between levels of a factor, especially when you want to be explicit about the reference; use name= when you need to access interaction coefficients or when piping into lfcShrink with apeglm.
section: Getting results — contrast specification

text:
Q: For a time-course RNA-seq experiment, why might a likelihood ratio test be more appropriate than the default Wald test in DESeq2?

A: The Wald test in DESeq2 tests whether a single coefficient is significantly different from zero (or from a threshold). In a time-course experiment, you typically have multiple time points and want to ask: does expression change over time at all, across any interval? Testing each time-point coefficient individually and then combining results leads to multiple comparison problems and misses genes with non-monotonic patterns (up then down, or vice versa). The likelihood ratio test (LRT) addresses this by comparing a full model that includes the time factor against a reduced model that omits it. The test statistic is the log-likelihood ratio between the two fits; the p-value reflects whether adding the time variable significantly improves model fit, integrating across all time points simultaneously. In DESeq2, this is run as DESeq(dds, test='LRT', reduced=~1) (or ~batch if batch is a covariate in the full model). The resulting p-values identify genes whose expression profile changes meaningfully over time without committing to a specific pattern. A critical point: the LFC values reported alongside LRT results are still taken from the full model coefficients and represent specific pairwise comparisons, not an overall 'change over time' effect — they should be interpreted cautiously or supplemented with a trajectory plot. The LRT is also useful for testing whether a batch effect or interaction term as a whole is significant.
section: Time course experiments — Likelihood ratio test

text:
Q: What are the trade-offs between vst and rlog for transforming DESeq2 counts before PCA or clustering?

A: Both vst (variance-stabilizing transformation) and rlog (regularized log transformation) transform raw counts so that variance is approximately constant across the range of mean expression, making them suitable for distance-based methods like PCA, hierarchical clustering, and heatmaps. Neither should ever be used as input to DE testing — that must always use raw counts. The main practical difference is computational: vst fits a parametric dispersion-mean trend and applies a closed-form transformation, making it very fast even for tens of thousands of samples. rlog fits a regularized log model that shrinks gene-level variance toward a common trend across samples; it is more computationally expensive and scales poorly beyond ~30 samples. For small datasets (fewer than ~30 samples), rlog is generally preferred because it handles genes with very low or zero counts more gracefully — the shrinkage prevents a handful of zero counts from producing extreme transformed values. For larger datasets, vst is the recommended default. Both transformations accept a blind=TRUE argument (default) that estimates dispersion without accounting for the experimental design, which is appropriate for exploratory quality control where you don't want to inadvertently use biological signal to determine outliers. Set blind=FALSE if you are performing the transformation after QC and want the dispersion estimate to reflect the actual design structure.
section: Data transformations — vst and rlog

text:
Q: DESeq2 automatically flags Cook's distance outliers and sets some p-values to NA. How does this work, and when should I adjust the minReplicatesForReplace setting?

A: Cook's distance measures the influence of a single observation on the overall model fit. DESeq2 computes Cook's distance for every count value (each sample × gene combination) after fitting the NB GLM. If the maximum Cook's distance for a gene exceeds a threshold (derived from an F-distribution percentile), DESeq2 flags that gene as having an outlier sample. What happens next depends on how many replicates are in the group containing the outlier. If the group has at least minReplicatesForReplace samples (default: 7), DESeq2 automatically replaces the outlier count with an imputed value — a trimmed-mean-based estimate — refits the model without the outlier, and reports results for that gene. This replacement approach is used when you have enough replicates that removing one observation still leaves a reliable fit. If the group has fewer than minReplicatesForReplace samples, DESeq2 cannot reliably impute, so it sets the gene's p-value and adjusted p-value to NA instead, removing it from the multiple testing pool. The default of 7 is deliberately conservative; most bulk RNA-seq studies have 3–6 replicates per group, meaning outlier genes will have NA p-values rather than automatic replacement. You might lower minReplicatesForReplace to 3 or 4 if you have moderate replication and want imputation to apply more broadly, but inspect the flagged genes manually first — a Cook's outlier sometimes indicates a sample swap or contamination that warrants removing the sample entirely rather than imputing.
section: Dealing with outliers — Cook's distance

text:
Q: I'm trying to decide between DADA2 and Deblur for denoising my 16S amplicon data. Both claim to produce exact sequence variants (ASVs). What are the practical differences, and when should I prefer one over the other?

A: Both DADA2 and Deblur produce ASVs rather than OTUs, avoiding the 97% clustering threshold that can lump distinct taxa together. However, they differ meaningfully in design. DADA2 uses a parametric error model learned from your specific run, making it highly adaptive to run-specific noise patterns — which is powerful but means joint analysis of data from different sequencing runs requires care (each run should be denoised independently before merging). Deblur uses a pre-computed, alignment-based error profile derived from PhiX control reads, which is less flexible but more consistent across datasets, making it simpler for combining data from multiple studies. In terms of read-length requirements, DADA2 supports paired-end joining, which allows you to recover longer amplicons, while Deblur operates on single-end (or already-joined) reads and requires trimming to a fixed uniform length, discarding positional length variation. DADA2's error learning also requires a sufficient number of reads per sample (QIIME 2 recommends at least 10,000 reads) to accurately parameterize the error model. Deblur can tolerate smaller per-sample depths because its error model is fixed. For typical single-run V4 datasets with paired-end reads, DADA2 is usually preferred for its sensitivity. For multi-study comparisons or single-end V3-V4 data, Deblur's consistency can be advantageous.
section: Tutorials — Atacama soil microbiome (DADA2) and Moving Pictures (Deblur)

text:
Q: Do I really need to trim primers before running DADA2 in QIIME 2? My reads already look clean in FastQC. What happens if I skip cutadapt?

A: Yes, primer trimming is required even if reads look clean in FastQC. Primer sequences are not biological amplicon signal — they are synthetic overhangs added during library preparation. If left in place, DADA2's error model will attempt to learn error rates across the primer region, which can distort the model because primers have artificially constrained sequence composition. More critically, primer bases will be incorporated into ASV sequences themselves, causing spurious mismatches during taxonomic classification and making ASVs incompatible with reference database sequences (which represent the amplicon region only, not primers). The cutadapt plugin in QIIME 2 (qiime cutadapt trim-paired or trim-single) handles primer removal before denoising. You should specify both the forward primer and the reverse-complement of the reverse primer for paired-end data. Reads where the primer is not detected can optionally be discarded with --p-discard-untrimmed, which is recommended to remove off-target amplification products. Even sequencing facilities that claim to strip primers computationally may leave partial primer fragments due to mismatches; running cutadapt yourself ensures clean input. Skipping trimming is one of the most common sources of inflated ASV counts and downstream classification failures in QIIME 2 workflows.
section: Tutorials — cutadapt: trimming adapters and primers

text:
Q: How do I choose --p-trunc-len-f and --p-trunc-len-r for DADA2? I've heard I should look at quality plots but I'm not sure how to interpret them or what threshold to use.

A: In QIIME 2, run qiime demux summarize on your demultiplexed reads to generate interactive quality plots before denoising. These plots show per-position median and quartile Phred quality scores. The goal when selecting trunc-len-f and trunc-len-r is to truncate reads at the position where quality drops below an acceptable floor — commonly around Q25-Q30 — while retaining enough length for paired-end reads to overlap sufficiently for merging. For paired-end V4 amplicons (~253 bp), a rough rule is that the sum of trunc-len-f and trunc-len-r should exceed the amplicon length by at least 20 bp to ensure adequate overlap for DADA2's merging step; if overlap is insufficient, merging rates will be very low and you will lose most reads. There is no single universal cutoff: the key trade-off is that aggressive truncation (high quality threshold) discards more signal and shortens reads, while lenient truncation retains low-quality bases that inflate error rates and reduce denoising accuracy. Examine your specific quality curves — Illumina 250×2 data typically degrades more on the reverse read, so asymmetric truncation lengths are common and appropriate. After running DADA2, inspect the denoising stats output (fraction of reads passing each step) to verify that merging and chimera removal rates are reasonable; poor merging usually indicates insufficient overlap from over-aggressive truncation.
section: Tutorials — DADA2 denoising; qiime demux summarize

text:
Q: I want to classify my ASVs taxonomically using QIIME 2's Naive Bayes classifier. Should I download a pre-trained classifier from the QIIME 2 website, or train my own? What are the consequences of getting this wrong?

A: The QIIME 2 documentation strongly recommends training a classifier on the exact same primer-trimmed, region-specific amplicon sequences you sequenced, rather than using a full-length pre-trained classifier. Pre-trained classifiers available on the QIIME 2 resources page are trained on the full-length 16S gene or on specific common primer pairs (e.g., 515F/806R for V4). If your primers differ even slightly, or if you sequenced a different variable region, the classifier's probability distributions will be mismatched to your reads and you will see degraded accuracy — typically more unclassified reads and incorrect species-level assignments. To train a custom classifier, use qiime feature-classifier fit-classifier-naive-bayes on reference sequences that have been extracted to your exact amplicon region using qiime feature-classifier extract-reads, specifying your actual forward and reverse primer sequences. This step extracts the primer-flanked region from SILVA or Greengenes2 reference sequences, and the resulting classifier is tuned to the exact length and composition of your target amplicon. The --p-confidence parameter (default 0.7) controls how aggressively uncertain predictions are collapsed to a higher taxonomic rank rather than reporting potentially wrong species assignments; lowering this threshold increases classification depth but also false positives, and raising it increases unclassified reads. For most studies, the default 0.7 is appropriate unless you have reason to prioritize depth over precision.
section: Tutorials — Training feature classifiers with q2-feature-classifier

text:
Q: My collaborator insists we rarefy our feature table before alpha and beta diversity analyses, but I've read that rarefaction throws away data. Is rarefaction still the recommended approach in QIIME 2?

A: Rarefaction remains the default approach in QIIME 2's core diversity workflow because it eliminates the confound introduced by highly variable sequencing depth across samples. When samples differ by an order of magnitude in read counts, richness metrics like observed features are trivially higher in deeply sequenced samples, and beta diversity distances can reflect depth differences rather than true community differences. Rarefaction addresses this by subsampling all samples to the same depth, making metrics directly comparable. The cost is genuine: samples below the rarefaction cutoff are dropped entirely, and reads above the cutoff are discarded. The qiime diversity alpha-rarefaction command lets you visualize how diversity metrics stabilize across a range of depths, helping you choose a sampling depth that retains most samples while reaching a plateau in diversity. Alternatives exist — proportional (relative abundance) normalization removes depth effects for beta diversity but inflates variance for low-count samples; CSS (cumulative sum scaling, implemented in q2-composition-related tools) is common in differential abundance contexts. Neither is universally better: for UniFrac-based beta diversity, rarefaction is standard; for compositional analysis with ANCOM or Songbird, rarefaction is generally not recommended because those methods operate on counts and have their own compositionality corrections. QIIME 2 does not enforce rarefaction — you can pass an unrarefied table to diversity metrics — but you must then account for depth variation yourself.
section: Tutorials — Alpha and beta diversity analysis; alpha-rarefaction

text:
Q: I'm running PERMANOVA with qiime diversity beta-group-significance to compare gut microbiome composition between treatment groups. A colleague told me I need to check for dispersion first. Why, and how do I do this in QIIME 2?

A: PERMANOVA tests whether the centroids of groups differ in multivariate space, but it is sensitive to differences in within-group dispersion (variance). If one group has much more spread-out samples than another, PERMANOVA can return a significant p-value even when centroids are identical — a false positive driven entirely by heterogeneous dispersion rather than a true difference in community composition. This is the dispersion confound. Before interpreting a significant PERMANOVA result, you should run qiime diversity beta-group-significance with --p-method permdisp, which implements the PERMDISP2 (betadisper) test. A significant PERMDISP result means groups differ in dispersion, and your PERMANOVA result may be unreliable or uninterpretable without accounting for this. In practice, both tests are often reported together: if PERMANOVA is significant and PERMDISP is not, you have stronger evidence of a true centroid difference. If both are significant, the PERMANOVA finding is confounded and should be interpreted cautiously — ideally with visual inspection of ordination plots. Additional PERMANOVA assumptions include exchangeability of samples under the null (approximately met with random assignment or matched designs) and independence of observations; pseudoreplication from repeated measures or nested designs violates this and requires a longitudinal or multilevel approach instead. QIIME 2's beta-group-significance also supports pairwise comparisons across multiple groups and stratification by a covariate, which can help disambiguate confounders.
section: Tutorials — Beta diversity analysis; qiime diversity beta-group-significance

text:
Q: What is Faith's Phylogenetic Diversity, and should I use it instead of observed features or Shannon entropy for alpha diversity? When does the choice of metric actually matter?

A: Faith's Phylogenetic Diversity (Faith's PD) sums the total branch length of the phylogenetic tree spanned by the ASVs present in a sample. Unlike observed features (which counts unique ASVs with equal weight) or Shannon entropy (which weights by abundance), Faith's PD incorporates evolutionary relationships — two communities with the same number of ASVs can have very different PD if one harbors deeply divergent lineages. Faith's PD is generally preferred when the phylogenetic breadth of diversity is biologically relevant — for example, comparing host-associated microbiomes across body sites or host species, or testing the 'phylogenetic signal' hypothesis. Observed features is the most interpretable but ignores both abundance and relatedness; it is strongly affected by rarefaction depth and sequencing noise. Shannon entropy captures richness and evenness but treats all ASVs as equidistant, which may overweight the contribution of closely related taxa. In QIIME 2, Faith's PD requires a rooted phylogenetic tree, which must be generated before calling qiime diversity core-metrics-phylogenetic. If you lack a good phylogenetic tree (e.g., non-16S or non-ITS markers without a reference tree), you should fall back to non-phylogenetic metrics. The choice matters most in comparative studies: if your groups differ in shallow taxa only, Faith's PD and observed features may both detect the difference, but if divergence patterns are key, only Faith's PD captures it. Reporting multiple complementary metrics is standard practice.
section: Tutorials — Alpha diversity; Faith's Phylogenetic Diversity

text:
Q: Should I use Bray-Curtis dissimilarity or UniFrac for beta diversity? My collaborator says weighted UniFrac is always better because it uses abundance information. Is that right?

A: The choice depends on your biological question, not on which metric is 'better.'
